title: Needle-felted elephant
subtitle: A standing grey merino elephant, 15 cm tall, with a curved trunk and shaped ears
excerpt: A standing needle-felted elephant in grey merino roving. Body 12 × 8 cm, four legs, a curved trunk that holds its shape, and large flat ears fanned forward. Tusks are optional in white merino. More involved than entry-level animals because the trunk requires careful progressive shaping.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted elephant figures are a common intermediate project in UK fibre arts teaching. Anatomy and construction approach synthesised from general needle felting practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, ready for needle felting or wet felting.
A barbed steel needle that tangles wool fibres together when stabbed through them repeatedly, binding them without heat or water.
A needle felting technique where a form is shaped gradually by working from the base outward and checking proportions before each stage is fully committed.
A wire or pipe-cleaner skeleton inside a needle-felted figure that supports poseable limbs. Not used in this project.

content.text: A needle-felted elephant is classed as intermediate because the trunk requires
progressive shaping
: it needs to be built up slowly over several passes to hold its curve without an
armature
. The body is a broad oval, 12 cm long and 8 cm wide at the widest point, and the finished standing height is approximately 15 cm to the top of the head. Grey merino
roving
is used throughout. The ears are large and flat, attached along their top and back edge and left fanning slightly forward. White merino tusks are optional and added last. A
felting needle
in a 36-gauge is used for initial shaping; a 38-gauge for detail; a 40-gauge for surface finishing.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

items.name: Merino roving, grey (mid or dark), 19-21 micron
Merino roving, white or ivory, for optional tusks
Foam felting mat, at least 10 cm thick

content.text: Method
Step 1: Form the body

Roll a large handful of grey roving into a dense oval. The unfelted oval should be about 18 cm long. Place on the foam mat and needle with the 36-gauge needle all over, rotating constantly, until the body is firm and measures 12 cm long and 8 cm in diameter at the widest point. The top surface should be slightly domed for the back; the underside flatter for the belly.
Step 2: Form the head

Roll a separate ball of grey roving slightly smaller than the body: needle it into a rounded cube shape approximately 7 cm across and 6 cm tall. The head of an elephant is nearly as large as the body and has a distinctive domed top. Needle the top face more firmly to create a slight dome. Do not attach to the body yet.
Step 3: Build the trunk using progressive shaping

Pull a length of roving about 20 cm long. Roll into a rope approximately 8 cm long and 2 cm in diameter. Needle on the mat until it holds a cylindrical shape but is not yet fully firm. Now curve it gently by placing it on the mat with the tip pointing upward and to one side, and needle the outer edge of the curve more than the inner edge: this creates differential density that locks the curve in place. Continue adding small amounts of roving to thicken the trunk base (where it meets the face) while keeping the tip thinner. Needle until the trunk holds its curve when held in the air.
Step 4: Attach the trunk to the head

Hold the trunk base against the centre lower face of the head. Needle through the trunk base into the head from multiple angles until the join is solid. The trunk should hang forward and down, curling slightly upward at the tip. If the trunk points downward without the upward curl, needle the underside of the tip more firmly.
Step 5: Make and attach the ears

For each ear, lay a generous wisp of grey roving flat on the foam mat and needle into a large rounded shape roughly 9 cm × 7 cm. Needle one face firmly, then flip and needle the other face. Leave the top and back edge slightly loose for attaching. Position each ear against the side of the head with the widest part of the ear hanging forward and outward from the face. Needle the top and back edge into the head firmly. The ear should fan forward, giving the elephant its characteristic wide silhouette.
Step 6: Attach the head to the body

Hold the head against the front of the body (the narrower end) and needle through the neck join from all angles. Add small pieces of roving to fill any gap at the join and needle them in. The neck should be sturdy: the head is heavy relative to the body and the join must hold the weight.
Step 7: Form and attach the legs

Make four cylindrical legs, each about 6 cm long and 2.5 cm in diameter. Needle each until firm. Attach to the underside of the body in pairs: front legs close to the head end, back legs at the rump. Needle through each leg into the body from the side and below. Check that all four feet touch a flat surface and the elephant stands level before fully securing.
Step 8: Optional tusks and surface finish

For tusks, roll two small lengths of white roving into slim curved cylinders about 5 cm long. Needle until firm and curve each one to a slight arc. Attach on each side of the trunk base, angling outward and slightly downward. Finally, work over the entire figure with the 40-gauge needle to smooth loose surface fibres. Pay particular attention to the joins around the trunk, ears, head, and legs.

items.problem: The trunk droops straight down and will not hold its curve
items.cause: The trunk was not needled with enough differential density on the outer curve, or it is too heavy
items.fix: Needle the outer edge of the desired curve more intensively while bracing the inner edge lightly. If the trunk is too heavy, reduce the roving amount and rebuild thinner.

items.problem: The head is wobbling at the neck join
items.cause: The neck area was not needled through from enough angles, or there is a gap between the head and body that was not filled
items.fix: Add a small coil of roving around the neck join and needle it in firmly from all sides to create a reinforced collar.

items.problem: The elephant does not stand without leaning
items.cause: Legs are of unequal length or positioned asymmetrically
items.fix: Detach the shortest leg, add roving to lengthen it, and re-attach. Check standing balance at each leg attachment step.

content.text: Care and finishing

Spot-clean with a barely damp cloth if dusty. Keep away from moisture. Display on a shelf or cabinet. The trunk is the most fragile element: avoid hanging the figure from the trunk or applying pressure to it.
